topic: rant about senior architect presenting her facade concept as his

turns.content: I need to vent rn or I'm going to lose it
Go ahead, I'm listening. What happened?

ok so you know how in architecture firms juniors do like 80% of the actual design work and seniors take the credit?? well today was a whole new level. I spent THREE WEEKS developing a parametric facade concept for this mixed-use project. custom algorithmic patterns, solar analysis, the whole thing. I even stayed late doing the grasshopper scripts myself because no one else on the team knows how. and this morning we had a client presentation and my senior presents MY facade concept as HIS. my slides. my renders. talking about "what I developed here" the whole time. I was sitting RIGHT THERE

That's infuriating. Three weeks of independent work including your own Grasshopper scripts -- that's not assisting on someone else's concept, that's original design. Did he acknowledge your contribution at all during the presentation, even as part of "the team"?

not even ONCE. not a single mention. tbh I kept waiting for him to say something like "Zara on my team did the computational work" or even just "we" instead of "I" but no. it was I I I the whole time. and the client LOVED it. they were asking all these questions about the solar responsiveness and he was answering them with information I literally put in the speaker notes for him
So he not only presented your concept as his own but used your speaker notes to field technical questions. That's a clear pattern. Did anyone else in the room know the work was yours?

my colleague Priya knows because she sits next to me and watched me build the whole thing. she gave me this LOOK during the meeting. but what am I supposed to do?? stand up in front of the client and say actually that's mine?? I'm junior. I've been here less than a year. ngl I feel so stupid for not seeing this coming

You're not stupid -- you did excellent work and expected basic professional integrity. The question now is how to protect yourself going forward. Start creating a paper trail: email files directly to your senior with subject lines like "Attached: facade concept I developed, let me know your thoughts." That timestamps your authorship. On the shared drive anyone can claim they contributed, but an email with your name on it is harder to erase.

I mean that's smart actually. I should have emailed the files instead of just putting them on the shared drive. ugh. I feel like I have to play office politics now instead of just designing. this is not what I went to architecture school for wallah. and tbh I'm scared if I say anything he'll gaslight me like "oh I thought we developed it together" and then I look difficult

The power dynamic makes direct confrontation risky, but there's a middle path: make yourself more visible without accusing anyone. Ask to present your own work at internal reviews. Volunteer for client-facing meetings. Build a reputation as the computational design person so that when parametric facades show up, everyone already associates them with you. Make it harder for anyone to claim your work without it being obvious.

you're right. I need to make myself undeniable. like if everyone knows I'm the parametric facade person then he can't just take it next time without people noticing. ok I'm going to start documenting everything and I'm going to ask my manager if I can do the next internal presentation myself. yalla let's see him try this again
That's the right energy. Document everything, build your visibility, and make your expertise impossible to overlook. The work speaks for itself -- you just need to make sure it speaks in your name.
